questions.question: Solve the recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n using the Master Theorem. Identify the values of a, b, and f(n), and determine which case of the Master Theorem applies.
keyPoints: Identify a=2, b=2, so n^log_b(a) = n^1 = n
Compare f(n) = n with n^log_b(a) = n
Since f(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a)), this is Case 2

questions.question: For the recurrence T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n², determine which case of the Master Theorem applies and justify your answer by comparing the polynomial relationship between f(n) and n^log_b(a).
keyPoints: Compare f(n) = n² with n^2: they are equal (polynomial equality)
This is Case 2 (regularity condition): f(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a) * log^k n) with k=0

questions.question: Solve the recurrence T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n² using the Master Theorem. Also explain the limitations of the Master Theorem, providing at least two scenarios where it cannot be applied with examples.
keyPoints: Step 1: Identify a=3, b=4, n^log_b(a) = n^log_4(3) ≈ n^0.793
Step 2: Compare f(n)=n² with n^0.793: f(n) grows faster (n² is polynomially larger)
Step 3: This is Case 3 (when f(n) = Ω(n^log_b(a)+ε) and regularity condition holds)

Limitations: (1) Cannot handle non-polynomial f(n) like n/log n, (2) Cannot handle varying a (non-constant subproblems), (3) Does not apply when T(n) is not defined for all n, (4) Cannot handle floors/ceilings in divide step

questions.question: Compare and contrast the three cases of the Master Theorem. For each case, provide a solved example recurrence showing when that case applies, and explain the polynomial comparison condition (ε > 0) required for distinguishing between Case 1 and Case 3.
keyPoints: Case 1: f(n) = O(n^log_b(a)-ε) - subproblem work dominates; example T(n)=4T(n/2)+1 gives Θ(n²)
Case 2: f(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a) * log^k n) - work at divide level dominates; example T(n)=2T(n/2)+n gives Θ(n log n)
Case 3: f(n) = Ω(n^log_b(a)+ε) with regularity condition - combining work dominates; example T(n)=3T(n/3)+n² gives Θ(n²)

Key distinction: Case 1 requires polynomial difference (ε > 0) where f(n) grows slower; Case 3 requires f(n) to grow faster by polynomial factor (ε > 0); Case 2 is the balanced polynomially equal case
